paper_title: Using a Diffusion Model for Pedestrian Trajectory Prediction in Semi-Open Autonomous Driving Environments

chunks.text:
[page 1]
17208 IEEE SENSORS JOURNAL, VOL. 24, NO. 10, 15 MAY 2024
Using a Diffusion Model for Pedestrian
Trajectory Prediction in Semi-Open
Autonomous Driving Environments
Yingjuan Tang
 , Hongwen He
 , Senior Member, IEEE, Y ong Wang
 , and Yifan Wu
Abstract—In recent years, the pervasive deployment and
progression of autonomous driving technology have engen-
dered heightened demands, particularly within the intricate
campus and surrounding environments frequently traversed
by autonomous delivery vehicles, such as automated food
delivery and courier services. Accurately predicting pedes-
trian trajectories is paramount in the realm of autonomous
driving. In the face of complex scenarios within campus
and surrounding environments, traditional pedestrian trajec-
tory prediction methods have failed to achieve satisfactory
results. To address this challenge systematically, this arti-
cle uses a digital twin methodology to establish a novel
dataset, denoted as the vulnerable pedestrian trajectory pre-
diction dataset (VPT), grounded in the authentic road network
structures of six campuses and their environs. This article
proposed a universal transformer diffusion modeling for vul-
nerable pedestrian trajectory prediction (UTD-PTP) trajectory
prediction framework based on the diffusion model, which
seeks to forecast pedestrian trajectories in settings char-
acterized by heightened pedestrian traffic, disorderliness,
and irregularities. Importantly, the applicability of our proposed methodology extends beyond campus environments,
showcasing commendable performance on standard autonomous driving datasets. Experimental results reveal an
average enhancement of 0.03 in ADE and 0.05 in FDE on publicly available datasets. On the VPT dataset, our method
demonstrates substantial impr

dataset. The input modality, denoted
as T + I, entails the concurrent utilization of both pedestrian
trajectory data and image data, while an input modality
of T signifies the usage of trajectory data exclusively. The
UTD-PTP method, as presented in this table, demonstrates
superior predictive efficacy across all 20 sample methods when
compared with its counterparts. On average, compared with the
leading SocialV AE [39], the method proposed in this article
shows improvements of 0.03 and 0.05 in terms of ADE and
FDE metrics, respectively. Moreover, the method introduced
in this study attains the highest performance on the majority
of subdatasets.
This article establishes a benchmark for VPT prediction in
semi-open campus scenarios based on the proposed dataset
VPT, as illustrated in Table IV. The LED [25] method serves
as a baseline model. SD-PTP is a pure diffusion prediction
model lacking the universal transformer architecture, instead
opting for the conventional transformer framework, while
UTD-PTP represents the complete model as presented in
this study. As delineated in Table IV, the results manifest
considerable improvements across ADE and FDE, through
the integration of our proposed UTD-PTP method, when
compared with the existing baseline model and SD-PTP.
C. Ablation Studies
In this section, we validate the efficacy of distinct modules
within our proposed UTD-PTP methodology through ablation
studies, as delineated in Figs. 3 and 4, and summarized in
Table II.

II. R ELATED WORK
A. T rajectory Forecasting Approaches for Pedestrian
Accurate pedestrian trajectory prediction enhances safety,
interactivity, and efficiency in the field of autonomous driving.
Currently, a substantial body of research on pedestrian trajec-
tory prediction exists, primarily divided into two directions:
physics-based prediction and data-driven prediction. Physics-
based modeling techniques, such as SFM [17], effectively
model pedestrian trajectories during the prediction process but
lack the flexibility to adapt to complex scenarios. Data-driven
Authorized licensed use limited to: China University of Petroleum. Downloaded on December 15,2025 at 07:00:08 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply.

[page 3]
17210 IEEE SENSORS JOURNAL, VOL. 24, NO. 10, 15 MAY 2024
methods excel in capturing inherent complexity in trajec-
tory interactions, thereby improving prediction accuracy [18].
However, deterministic data-driven methods such as GRU [19]
and MSFM [5] struggle to model the uncertainty of pedes-
trian trajectories. In semi-closed campuses and surrounding
scenarios with narrow roads and complex network structures,
this environment poses greater challenges for deterministic
pedestrian trajectory prediction. Recently, TRACE [20], based
on the diffusion model, has shown outstanding performance
in closed-loop pedestrian trajectory generation. Motivated by
this, this article proposes a novel approach using deep gen-
erative models [21] and the diffusion model to encapsulate
the complex process supporting the modeling of uncertainty
variations in pedestrian trajectory distributions in semi-open
scenarios. This work pioneers the application of the diffusion
model in predicting the trajectories of vulnerable pedestrians
in semi-open scenari

g safety [27]. The existing corpus of
pedestrian trajectory prediction datasets is predominantly con-
centrated within conventional scenarios, encompassing open
roadways, junctions, and roundabouts [28]. Conversely, there
exists a scarcity of datasets that systematically address the
intricacies of pedestrian trajectory prediction in semi-open
environments. With the advancement of autonomous driving
technology, the demand for autonomous delivery vehicles,
such as automated food delivery and courier services in
semi-open scenarios, is increasing. This study introduces a
novel dataset specifically designed for predicting pedestrian
trajectories in semi-open scenarios (campus and surrounding
residential areas).
III. P ROPOSED METHOD
In this section, we present the UTD-PTP, an innovative
approach for trajectory forecasting that integrates a univer-
sal transformer within a diffusion model to enhance the
accuracy of human trajectory prediction. We first explicitly
formulate the uncertainty variation as an inverse diffusion
process based on DDIMs. We then describe how, compared
with the traditional diffusion models based on the U-net
network [29], this article innovatively introduces the universal
transformer architecture for the proposed UTD-PTP method.
Finally, we describe how to train this diffusion model.
A. Problem Formulation
To predict the trajectory of a target agent A0, we use the
input history trajectory XA0 = [xa0
−tp+1, xa0
−tp+2, . . . ,xa0
0] ∈
Rtp×2, which represents the observed past trajectory over
tp timestamps. Here, xa0t ∈ R2 captures the 2-D spa-
tial coordinates at timestamp t. The surrounding pedestrian
trajectories of other agents can be formulated as Xn =
[XA1 , XA2 , XA3 , . . . ,XAn ], where the A1, A2, A3, . . . ,An is the
surrounding pedestrian age
